title: The Tiger King by Kalki
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: Abuse of power, pride, fate, and satire on rulers who misuse power for vanity. The story also suggests that harming nature backfires.

summary:
This is a satire on the arrogance of those in power. The Maharaja of Pratibandapuram is called the Tiger King. When he was born, astrologers predicted that he would die one day. The baby king, just ten days old, surprisingly spoke and asked how he would die. The astrologers said a tiger would be the cause. The young prince declared, “Let tigers beware!”

When he turns twenty, the Maharaja becomes king and starts killing tigers. He kills the first one and proudly shows it to the astrologer. The astrologer says he may kill ninety-nine tigers, but he must be careful with the hundredth. The king vows to kill one hundred tigers. He starts hunting and kills seventy tigers in ten years.

But then the tiger population in his state ends. To kill more, he marries into a state with many tigers. He kills ninety-nine tigers in total and becomes desperate for the hundredth. When a tiger is reported in his forests, he goes to hunt, but the tiger seems to have vanished. The angry king fires officers. To save their jobs, the officers bring an old, weak tiger from the zoo. The king shoots it but misses. The tiger faints from shock. The hunters kill it themselves so the king thinks he has killed the hundredth tiger.

The king is thrilled, but his death comes from a wooden toy tiger. On his son’s third birthday, he buys a cheap wooden tiger. While playing with it, a tiny wooden sliver pierces his right hand. The infection spreads, and three famous surgeons cannot save him. The Maharaja dies. The irony is that the king kills ninety-nine real tigers but is killed by a toy tiger, the hundredth tiger.

qa.q: Who is the Tiger King? Why does he get that name?
qa.a: The Tiger King is the Maharaja of Pratibandapuram, Sir Jilani Jung Jung Bahadur. He gets this name because he becomes obsessed with killing tigers after astrologers predict that his death will come from a tiger.

qa.q: What did the royal infant grow up to be?
qa.a: The royal infant grew up to become the Maharaja of Pratibandapuram. At the age of twenty, the state came into his hands, and he began his tiger-hunting mission.

qa.q: What will the Maharaja do to find the required number of tigers to kill?
qa.a: When tigers became fewer in his own state, the Maharaja decided to marry a princess from a state with a large tiger population. After marriage, he visited his father-in-law’s state and killed five or six tigers each time until he reached ninety-nine tigers.

qa.q: How will the Maharaja prepare himself for the hundredth tiger which was supposed to decide his fate?
qa.a: The Maharaja became extremely anxious and careful when only one tiger remained. He searched desperately for the hundredth tiger because he believed killing it would save him from the astrologer’s prediction.

qa.q: What will now happen to the astrologer? Do you think the prophecy was indisputably disproved?
qa.a: The astrologer would not have to cut off his tuft, crop his hair, burn his books, or become an insurance agent because the prophecy was not disproved. The hundredth tiger finally caused the Maharaja’s death indirectly through the wooden toy tiger.

qa.q: The story is a satire on the conceit of those in power. How does the author employ the literary device of dramatic irony in the story?
qa.a: The author uses dramatic irony by showing that the Tiger King tries to escape death by killing tigers, but finally dies because of a wooden tiger. He believes he has defeated fate, but the reader understands that his pride and cruelty lead him towards the prophecy’s fulfilment.

qa.q: What is the author’s indirect comment on subjecting innocent animals to the wilfulness of human beings?
qa.a: The author indirectly criticises the cruelty of humans who kill innocent animals for pride, pleasure, and superstition. The Maharaja’s hunting is not for need or protection but for his personal fear and ego. The story shows how animals suffer because of human arrogance.

qa.q: How would you describe the behaviour of the Maharaja’s minions towards him? Do you find them truly sincere towards him or are they driven by fear when they obey him? Do we find a similarity in today’s political order?
qa.a: The Maharaja’s ministers and officers obey him mostly out of fear, not sincerity. They flatter him and follow even foolish orders because they are afraid of punishment. A similar attitude can sometimes be seen today when people around powerful leaders agree with them blindly for position, favour, or fear.

qa.q: Can you relate instances of game-hunting among the rich and the powerful in the present times that illustrate the callousness of human beings towards wildlife?
qa.a: Yes. Even today, some rich and powerful people hunt animals illegally or for trophies. Such acts show cruelty and lack of respect for wildlife. They treat animals as objects of pride instead of living beings that deserve protection.

qa.q: We need a new system for the age of ecology — a system which is embedded in the care of all people and also in the care of the Earth and all life upon it. Discuss.
qa.a: Yes, we need a system that protects both human beings and nature. Human progress should not destroy animals, forests, rivers, and the environment. Laws, education, and social values should promote compassion, ecological balance, wildlife protection, and responsible use of natural resources.
